      Trump SPAC: Background of the Transactions
   =20
 =20
The way a special purpose acquisition company works is that a sponsor raise=
s a bunch of money and then goes out and looks for a private company to tak=
e public using that money. In theory anyone could do this. I could launch a=
 SPAC, the SPAC Stuff Acquisition Co., and try to raise money from the read=
ers of this column. Maybe it would work. Then I=E2=80=99d have some money i=
n the bank, and then I=E2=80=99d go out and look for a company to acquire. =
How would I do that? I don=E2=80=99t know. I don=E2=80=99t personally know =
anyone who runs a big private company that they=E2=80=99d like to take publ=
ic. I suppose I could call up some investment bankers and say =E2=80=9Chey =
do you know any companies that would like to merge with a SPAC?=E2=80=9D Bu=
t I gather that hundreds of SPACs are calling those bankers every day and, =
you know, wait times are long.=C2=A0In practice the people who launch SPACs=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0and particularly the people who launch successful SPACs =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0tend to be people who know a lot of people who run companies th=
at they might want to take public. Sometimes these SPAC sponsors=C2=A0are s=
uccessful entrepreneurs or operators with good networks, but often they are=
 private-equity or venture-capital investors, or activist hedge fund manage=
rs, or mergers-and-acquisitions investment bankers. They are people who are=
 in the deals business, people whose experience is in=C2=A0buying companies=
 or doing something closely adjacent. Every day they go to work in the comp=
any-buying business. Maybe they run a private-equity fund that buys compani=
es and a family office that buys other companies and a credit fund that len=
ds to other people who buy companies; whenever they meet with a company the=
y think =E2=80=9Cwhich pocket should I buy this company from?=E2=80=9D=C2=
=A0The SPAC is just another tool, another pool of cash, another source of f=
ees, for their basic business of buying companies.If you=E2=80=99re a perso=
n like that, the day before you raise a SPAC you probably had=C2=A0lunch wi=
th someone who has a company that you might buy. The day after you raise th=
e SPAC you have drinks with someone who has a company that you might buy. O=
ver the weekend you go golfing with someone who has a company you might buy=
. You spend your time talking to people with companies about maybe buying t=
heir companies.When you do the SPAC, your lawyer will write the prospectus,=
 and she will sit you down and say =E2=80=9CLook, it is a rule of SPACs tha=
t you have to be a genuinely blank-check company. You can=E2=80=99t start a=
 SPAC with a deal already signed up, or even with discussions with a target=
 that are far along. If you=E2=80=99re in substantive discussions with one =
company about taking it public using this SPAC, you can=E2=80=99t do the SP=
AC. You=E2=80=99d have to at least disclose those discussions, but in pract=
ice then the Securities and Exchange Commission would want you to include d=
etailed information about the target, and people would be nervous about buy=
ing it, and it would be a huge mess and the target company might as well do=
 a regular initial public offering instead of bothering with a SPAC deal. S=
o: Are you in any substantive discussions with any companies about merging =
them with your SPAC?=E2=80=9DAnd you will say, well, all I ever do in my li=
fe is talk to companies about acquiring them or taking them public or whate=
ver. Have I mentioned to some of them that I am doing a SPAC and maybe it w=
ould be a good vehicle for them? Sure, probably, I can=E2=80=99t remember a=
ll the companies I=E2=80=99ve talked to this week. But I haven=E2=80=99t, l=
ike,=C2=A0signed any deals=C2=A0for the SPAC. It=E2=80=99s probably fine.An=
d your lawyer is like, sure, it=E2=80=99s probably fine. And you take the S=
PAC public.And then a month later you sign a deal with a company you had be=
en talking to before you took the SPAC public and everyone is like, well, t=
hat=E2=80=99s life in the deals business, you talk to a lot of companies al=
l the time.There is obviously a gray area here. If you take a SPAC public a=
nd you are truly talking to only=C2=A0one=C2=A0private company to take publ=
ic, and it is clearly the apple of your SPAC=E2=80=99s eye and the only dea=
l you really want to do, and your discussions are far along, and then short=
ly after the SPAC goes public you sign a deal with that target you have bee=
n talking to, then that=E2=80=99s not great. But it is not a particularly b=
right line. If you=E2=80=99re in the deals business you might well have tal=
ked to a company about deals before, and then when you have a SPAC you migh=
t do a SPAC deal with that company, and that=E2=80=99s, you know, fine-ish.=
Last month a SPAC called Digital World Acquisition Corp. signed a deal with=
 Donald Trump=E2=80=99s more or less nonexistent social media/streaming/web=
-hosting/digital-payments=C2=A0company Trump Media & Technology Group, and =
I  made fun of it a lot and =E2=80=A6=C2=A0let=E2=80=99s say that I am not =
yet convinced that TMTG has a well-thought-out strategy for building a soci=
al media empire or getting into the digital payments space. But here is a N=
ew York Times story about the SPAC that I cannot get all that worked up abo=
ut:To get his deal done, Mr. Trump ventured into an unregulated and sometim=
es shadowy corner of Wall Street, working with an unlikely cast of characte=
rs: the former =E2=80=9CApprentice=E2=80=9D contestants, a small Chinese in=
vestment firm and a little-known Miami banker named Patrick Orlando.Mr. Orl=
ando had been discussing a deal with Mr. Trump since at least March, accord=
ing to people familiar with the talks and a confidential investor presentat=
ion reviewed by The New York Times. That was well before his SPAC, Digital =
World Acquisition, made its debut on the Nasdaq stock exchange last month. =
In doing so, Mr. Orlando=E2=80=99s SPAC may have skirted securities laws an=
d stock exchange rules, lawyers said.SPACs sell their shares to investors t=
hrough an initial public offering and then find a private company with whic=
h to merge. Because SPACs are empty vessels, stock exchanges allow them to =
list their shares without disclosing much financial information. But that c=
reates opportunities for SPACs to serve as backdoor vehicles for companies =
to go public without receiving the kind of investor scrutiny they would in =
a traditional listing. To prevent that, SPACs aren=E2=80=99t supposed to ha=
ve a merger planned at the time of their I.P.O.Lawyers and industry officia=
ls said that talks between Mr. Orlando and Mr. Trump or their associates co=
nsequently could draw scrutiny from the Securities and Exchange Commission.=
Another issue is that Digital World=E2=80=99s securities filings repeatedly=
 stated that the company and its executives had not engaged in any =E2=80=
=9Csubstantive discussions, directly or indirectly,=E2=80=9D with a target =
company =E2=80=94 even though Mr. Orlando had been in discussions with Mr. =
Trump.Given the politically fraught nature of a deal with Mr. Trump, securi=
ties lawyers said that Digital World=E2=80=99s lack of disclosure about tho=
se conversations could be considered an omission of =E2=80=9Cmaterial infor=
mation.=E2=80=9DBut they weren=E2=80=99t talking about merging Trump Imagin=
ary Thing with DWAC, at least not at first. They were talking about merging=
 it with a different, already-public SPAC:By the time they started working =
together in the winter of 2021, Mr. Orlando already had three SPACs trading=
 on U.S. stock exchanges.One of them, Benessere Capital Acquisition, had go=
ne public on Jan. 7 =E2=80=94 the day after Mr. Trump=E2=80=99s supporters =
rioted at the Capitol =E2=80=94 and raised about $100 million. Mr. Orlando =

=9D=C2=A0I sometimes think that there is a  certain wishfulness in the assu=

econd wallet to the first in exchange for one WashCoin.=09The next day, you=

hat one WashCoin.=09Now the trading price of a WashCoin is $2, so its total=
 market capitalization is $2 trillion, up 100% in the last 24 hours.=09You =
still own 100% of the WashCoins and nobody has paid you anything for them.=

ight.Is this legal? In the stock market, no, of course not; it is =E2=80=9C=
wash trading.=E2=80=9D Is it legal in the crypto market? I dunno man, it=E2=
=80=99s crypto. Does this describe all of crypto? No, of course not, Bitcoi=
n trading is pretty much all independent economic activity and very little =
wash trading at all. Does it describe =E2=80=A6=C2=A0a certain amount =E2=
=80=A6=C2=A0of crypto? Yes, absolutely. If you see that a ridiculous non-fu=
ngible token sold for $500 million, should you just assume that the buyer w=
as also the seller?  Probably!A white-haired, green-eyed pixelated characte=
r known as a CryptoPunk 9998 just sold for more than half a billion U.S. do=
llars -- or so it appeared -- the latest wild development in the booming no=

e NFT trade ended up right back where it started, raising the question of w=
hy anyone bothered. =E2=80=A6In conventional, regulated securities markets,=
 this would be called wash trading, which is banned on grounds that trading=

 million, then other CryptoPunks are also worth a lot. But also=C2=A0other,=
 non-CryptoPunk NFTs are more attractive, because if CryptoPunks are so val=
uable then that process might repeat. And=C2=A0Ethereum=C2=A0is more attrac=
tive, as a platform for buying NFTs. And=C2=A0other cryptocurrencies=C2=A0a=
re more valuable, because look how quickly Ethereum=E2=80=99s ecosystem dev=
eloped, maybe that can be repeated. Everyone who is already in the broad cr=
ypto ecosystem benefits when stuff in crypto gets more valuable, because th=

 natural light. But  it=E2=80=99s a game of tradeoffs.=E2=80=9D There=E2=80=
=99s a butler shortage. Pablo Escobar=E2=80=99s =E2=80=98cocaine hippos=E2=
=80=99 are legally people, US court rules.=C2=A0If you'd like to get=C2=A0M=
oney=C2=A0Stuff=C2=A0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please=C2=A0=
subscribe at this link. Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great=
 Bloomberg newsletters here. Thanks![1] I will say, it would be funny if th=
ey were called =E2=80=9CAlphabetizers.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CAbecedarians.=E2=
=80=9D=C2=A0[2] I realize that ETFs don=E2=80=99t quite work that way but, =
come on, there's a price.[3] I have  written about Axie Infinity before. =
=E2=80=9CMostly this just makes me feel old,=E2=80=9D I said. Still does!

<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Trump SPAC: Background of the Transactions</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The way a special purpose acquisition =
company works is that a sponsor raises a bunch of money and then goes out a=
nd looks for a private company to take public using that money. In theory a=
nyone could do this. I could launch a SPAC, the SPAC Stuff Acquisition Co.,=
 and try to raise money from the readers of this column. Maybe it would wor=
k. Then I=E2=80=99d have some money in the bank, and then I=E2=80=99d go ou=
t and look for a company to acquire. How would I do that? I don=E2=80=99t k=
now. I don=E2=80=99t personally know anyone who runs a big private company =
that they=E2=80=99d like to take public. I suppose I could call up some inv=
estment bankers and say =E2=80=9Chey do you know any companies that would l=
ike to merge with a SPAC?=E2=80=9D But I gather that <a href=3D"https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25541041.270650/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNv=
bS9hcnRpY2xlcy9zcGFjLXB1bGxiYWNrLXByZXNzdXJlcy1jcmVhdG9ycy10by1maW5kLXF1YWx=
pdHktbWVyZ2Vycy0xMTYyMjQ5NDU2NA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe270392c" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
hundreds of SPACs</a> are calling those bankers every day and, you know, wa=
it times are long.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In practice the pe=
ople who launch SPACs =E2=80=94=C2=A0and particularly the people who launch=
 successful SPACs =E2=80=94=C2=A0tend to be people who know a lot of people=
 who run companies that they might want to take public. Sometimes these SPA=
C sponsors=C2=A0are successful entrepreneurs or operators with good network=
s, but often they are private-equity or venture-capital investors, or activ=
ist hedge fund managers, or mergers-and-acquisitions investment bankers. Th=
ey are people who are in the deals business, people whose experience is in=
=C2=A0buying companies or doing something closely adjacent. Every day they =
go to work in the company-buying business. Maybe they run a private-equity =
fund that buys companies and a family office that buys other companies and =
a credit fund that lends to other people who buy companies; whenever they m=
eet with a company they think =E2=80=9Cwhich pocket should I buy this compa=
ny from?=E2=80=9D=C2=A0The SPAC is just another tool, another pool of cash,=
 another source of fees, for their basic business of buying companies.</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you=E2=80=99re a person like that, the day b=
efore you raise a SPAC you probably had=C2=A0lunch with someone who has a c=
ompany that you might buy. The day after you raise the SPAC you have drinks=
 with someone who has a company that you might buy. Over the weekend you go=
 golfing with someone who has a company you might buy. You spend your time =
talking to people with companies about maybe buying their companies.</p><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When you do the SPAC, your lawyer will write the =
prospectus, and she will sit you down and say =E2=80=9CLook, it is a rule o=
f SPACs that you have to be a genuinely blank-check company. You can=E2=80=
=99t start a SPAC with a deal already signed up, or even with discussions w=
ith a target that are far along. If you=E2=80=99re in substantive discussio=
ns with one company about taking it public using this SPAC, you can=E2=80=
=99t do the SPAC. You=E2=80=99d have to at least disclose those discussions=
, but in practice then the Securities and Exchange Commission would want yo=
u to include detailed information about the target, and people would be ner=
vous about buying it, and it would be a huge mess and the target company mi=
ght as well do a regular initial public offering instead of bothering with =
a SPAC deal. So: Are you in any substantive discussions with any companies =
about merging them with your SPAC?=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"=
>And you will say, well, all I ever do in my life is talk to companies abou=
t acquiring them or taking them public or whatever. Have I mentioned to som=
e of them that I am doing a SPAC and maybe it would be a good vehicle for t=
hem? Sure, probably, I can=E2=80=99t remember all the companies I=E2=80=99v=
e talked to this week. But I haven=E2=80=99t, like,=C2=A0<em>signed any dea=
ls=C2=A0</em>for the SPAC. It=E2=80=99s probably fine.</p><p style=3D"margi=
n: 16px 0;">And your lawyer is like, sure, it=E2=80=99s probably fine. And =
you take the SPAC public.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And then a month =
later you sign a deal with a company you had been talking to before you too=
k the SPAC public and everyone is like, well, that=E2=80=99s life in the de=
als business, you talk to a lot of companies all the time.</p><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">There is obviously a gray area here. If you take a SPAC pub=
lic and you are truly talking to only=C2=A0<em>one=C2=A0</em>private compan=
y to take public, and it is clearly the apple of your SPAC=E2=80=99s eye an=
d the only deal you really want to do, and your discussions are far along, =
and then shortly after the SPAC goes public you sign a deal with that targe=
t you have been talking to, then that=E2=80=99s not great. But it is not a =
particularly bright line. If you=E2=80=99re in the deals business you might=
 well have talked to a company about deals before, and then when you have a=
 SPAC you might do a SPAC deal with that company, and that=E2=80=99s, you k=
now, fine-ish.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Last month a SPAC called Dig=
ital World Acquisition Corp. signed a deal with Donald Trump=E2=80=99s more=
 or less nonexistent social media/streaming/web-hosting/digital-payments=C2=
=A0company Trump Media & Technology Group, and I <a href=3D"https://lin=
k.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25541041.270650/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21i=
ZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjEtMTAtMjEvbWF0dC1sZXZpbmUtcy1tb25leS1=
zdHVmZi1kb25hbGQtdHJ1bXAtZG9lcy1hLXNwYWM_Y21waWQ9QkJEMTEwMTIxX01PTkVZU1RVRk=
YmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjExMTAxJ=
nV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB8f83144e" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
 made fun of it a lot</a> and =E2=80=A6=C2=A0let=E2=80=99s say that I am no=
t yet convinced that TMTG has a well-thought-out strategy for building a so=
cial media empire or getting into the digital payments space. But here is a=
 <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/25541041.270650/a=
HR0cHM6Ly93d3cubnl0aW1lcy5jb20vMjAyMS8xMC8yOS9idXNpbmVzcy90cnVtcC1zcGFjLWRp=
Z2l0YWwtd29ybGQuaHRtbA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB0ae5dad9" itemprop=3D"Story=
Link" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">New York =
Times story</a> about the SPAC that I cannot get all that worked up about:<=
/p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">To get his deal done, Mr. Trump=
 ventured into an unregulated and sometimes shadowy corner of Wall Street, =
working with an unlikely cast of characters: the former =E2=80=9CApprentice=
=E2=80=9D contestants, a small Chinese investment firm and a little-known M=
iami banker named Patrick Orlando.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Mr. Orla=
ndo had been discussing a deal with Mr. Trump since at least March, accordi=
ng to people familiar with the talks and a confidential investor presentati=
on reviewed by The New York Times. That was well before his SPAC, Digital W=
orld Acquisition, made its debut on the Nasdaq stock exchange last month. I=
n doing so, Mr. Orlando=E2=80=99s SPAC may have skirted securities laws and=
 stock exchange rules, lawyers said.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">SPACs =
sell their shares to investors through an initial public offering and then =
find a private company with which to merge. Because SPACs are empty vessels=
, stock exchanges allow them to list their shares without disclosing much f=
inancial information. But that creates opportunities for SPACs to serve as =
backdoor vehicles for companies to go public without receiving the kind of =
investor scrutiny they would in a traditional listing. To prevent that, SPA=
Cs aren=E2=80=99t supposed to have a merger planned at the time of their I.=
P.O.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Lawyers and industry officials said th=
at talks between Mr. Orlando and Mr. Trump or their associates consequently=
 could draw scrutiny from the Securities and Exchange Commission.</p><p sty=
le=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Another issue is that Digital World=E2=80=99s securi=
ties filings repeatedly stated that the company and its executives had not =
engaged in any =E2=80=9Csubstantive discussions, directly or indirectly,=E2=
=80=9D with a target company =E2=80=94 even though Mr. Orlando had been in =
discussions with Mr. Trump.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Given the polit=
ically fraught nature of a deal with Mr. Trump, securities lawyers said tha=
t Digital World=E2=80=99s lack of disclosure about those conversations coul=
d be considered an omission of =E2=80=9Cmaterial information.=E2=80=9D</p><=
/blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But they <em>weren=E2=80=99t</em> =
talking about merging Trump Imaginary Thing with DWAC, at least not at firs=
t. They were talking about merging it with a different, already-public SPAC=
:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">By the time they started work=
ing together in the winter of 2021, Mr. Orlando already had three SPACs tra=
ding on U.S. stock exchanges.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One of them, =
Benessere Capital Acquisition, had gone public on Jan. 7 =E2=80=94 the day =
after Mr. Trump=E2=80=99s supporters rioted at the Capitol =E2=80=94 and ra=
ised about $100 million. Mr. Orlando created Benessere with the help of a S=
hanghai-based firm called ARC Capital that specialized in helping Chinese c=
ompanies list on U.S. stock exchanges. ARC kicked in funding for Benessere.=
</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On Feb. 8, Trump Media was incorporated in=
 Delaware.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">By March, Mr. Orlando and Mr. Tr=
ump were discussing a merger of Trump Media and Benessere, according to peo=
ple with knowledge of the talks who were not authorized to discuss it publi=
cly. The investor presentation about the planned deal envisioned the combin=
ed company, which would offer a social media app, films, events and eventua=
lly a variety of technology services, being worth $15 billion and rivaling =
tech giants like Netflix and the cloud divisions of Amazon and Google.</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At some point, Benessere=E2=80=99s attractivene=
ss as a financing vehicle for Mr. Trump=E2=80=99s venture faded, in part be=
cause its roughly $100 million war chest was considered inadequate, accordi=
ng to a person briefed on the matter. (Benessere is still looking for a com=
pany to buy.)</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But Mr. Orlando had another, =
bigger SPAC that was preparing for liftoff. In May, Digital World announced=
 plans for an I.P.O.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If the st=
ory here is =E2=80=9CPatrick Orlando is a deal guy and has a bunch of SPACs=
, he talked to Trump about merging one of them with TMTG, that didn=E2=80=
=99t work out, but then he had another one and merged that with TMTG,=E2=80=
=9D that seems more or less fine? If the story here is =E2=80=9CPatrick Orl=
ando talked to Trump about merging a SPAC with TMTG, Trump was like =E2=80=
=98that doesn=E2=80=99t work but do a bigger SPAC and we=E2=80=99ll merge w=
ith that one,=E2=80=99 and so Orlando launched a bigger SPAC specifically t=
o merge with TMTG,=E2=80=9D then that=E2=80=99s bad, you can=E2=80=99t do t=
hat. From the outside, those things are not=C2=A0<em>that=C2=A0</em>differe=
nt.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One question you might ask here is: If =
they did the bad thing (take a SPAC public with prearranged plans to merge =
with TMTG), what is the remedy for that? One answer is of course that the S=
ecurities and Exchange Commission can sue them and collect a fine.=C2=A0But=
 the main remedy for bad securities disclosure is generally giving investor=
s their money back, or rather, giving investors (1) complete accurate discl=
osure plus (2)=C2=A0the option to get their money back. This is a weird rem=
edy because:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; =
margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">SPACs=C2=A0<em>always=
=C2=A0</em>offer investors their money back when they announce their deal. =
That=E2=80=99s just how SPACs work: They announce a deal, then they file a =
prospectus with complete disclosure about the company they=E2=80=99re mergi=
ng with, and then their investors can redeem their stock for the $10 they p=
ut in=C2=A0(plus interest) if they don=E2=80=99t like the deal.</li>=09<li =
style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">DWAC is way way way up from its initial publi=
c offering price =E2=80=94=C2=A0it went public at $10 and closed on Friday =
at $67.75 =E2=80=94=C2=A0so no one will want their money back.</li></ol><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s worth dwelling on Point 1 for a minu=
te because it is a structural solution to the problem of a SPAC going publi=
c with a secret deal lined up. The idea is =E2=80=9Cyou should not be able =
to raise money from public markets=C2=A0<em>for a specific company=C2=A0</e=
m>without giving investors complete disclosure=C2=A0<em>about that company<=
/em>.=E2=80=9D And the solution is, you can raise money for a SPAC, but the=
n once you announce the deal you have to give investors complete disclosure=
 and=C2=A0<em>let them take their money back</em>. You=E2=80=99re still not=
 allowed to do a SPAC with a secret deal lined up, but if you do that anywa=
y, the harm to investors is pretty minimal due to other safeguards.</p><p s=
tyle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The=C2=A0<em>actual harm=C2=A0</em>here, if=C2=A0D=
WAC and TMTG secretly had a deal lined up when DWAC went public, was to peo=
ple who bought DWAC=E2=80=99s stock at $10 and flipped it at $10.05. They b=
ought a SPAC IPO for $10, they held it a few days, they thought =E2=80=9Cme=
h this is just a random lottery ticket, making 5 cents in a few days is fin=
e, no need to hold onto this,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0and then it turned out that the=
 thing they let go of was actually a sweet sweet Trump meme stock. If they =
had known from the beginning that this would be a Trump SPAC, they would no=
t have sold. I=C2=A0suppose they should sue? But it would be a bit weird fo=
r <em>the SEC</em> to stand up for them: =E2=80=9CYou should have told inve=
stors=C2=A0that you were planning to buy Donald Trump=E2=80=99s totally hyp=
othetical social media company, because they=E2=80=99d want to get in on th=
e hype cycle for that.=E2=80=9D Is that =E2=80=A6=C2=A0is that how we prese=
rve confidence in the fairness of financial markets?</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t know! I guess my two conclusions about this story=
 are:</p><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin:=
 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Mainly I was surprised that =
TMTG has been kicking around this long? It was pitched to Trump, by =E2=80=
=9Ctwo former contestants on his reality show, =E2=80=98The Apprentice,=E2=
=80=99=E2=80=9D in January 2021, and the company was incorporated in Februa=
ry. It was not formed two weeks ago purely to cash in on SPAC money; this i=
s a thing that somebody at least vaguely wants to do.</li>=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">If they did the bad thing, I suppose that=E2=80=99s a s=
ort of fraud, but it is a somewhat arcane sort of fraud. How many times do =
you think=C2=A0I will write that sentence about the Trump SPAC? I just feel=
 like we are all going to learn a lot about all sorts of arcane frauds and =
I am excited to go on this journey with you and Trump SPAC.</li></ol><table=

ackground-color: #ccc;">Brown Assets for the Prudent Investor</a>=E2=80=9D =
by Alon Brav and J.B. Heaton:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">M=
ost commentary on climate-themed investment treats climate change as a one-=
way risk to brown assets from a hoped-for transition to a low-carbon econom=
y. But the converse holds as well. Brown assets could turn out to be highly=
 valuable if the world fails to transition out of the high-carbon economy. =
This is true both because sentiment for green assets may cause brown assets=
 to be underpriced (generating higher expected returns) and because brown a=
ssets may provide a valuable hedge against the costs of climate change in a=
 world that failed to transition to a low-carbon economy. Given the lack of=
 progress to date toward transition to a low-carbon economy, we argue that =
institutional investors subject to fiduciary duties of prudent investment (=
including the duty to diversify) cannot yet justify divestment from brown a=
ssets.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A year ago, you could h=

 gets more valuable, because that entices new people into the crypto ecosys=
tem and makes all of the stuff more valuable.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=

=80=99s corner of the metaverse, primitive as it may be now, players must b=
uy NFTs =E2=80=94 the blobby monsters called Axies =E2=80=94 before they ca=

r in Ethereum, the No. 2 cryptocurrency after bitcoin. In other words, it t=
akes nearly a grand to start playing, with no guarantees of success.</p><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The result: Axie has become the single most valua=

r video games with live streams and chat functionality.=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Because Axie NFTs are so expensive, Peurifoy funds =E2=

ifoy then splits the winnings with his players. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">Carlos Enrique Sierra Almaraz, 24, discovered Axie this summ=
er and says he=E2=80=99s dropped out of medical school in hopes of getting =

o playing for several hours a day, he also handles various administrative t=
